coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots as nanosensors for the spectrofluorimetric determination of meloxicam in biological fluids and dosage forms: Greenness assessment and application to content uniformity testing

coredata.dc:description: Highly fluorescent water-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal-assisted synthesis procedure utilizing ascorbic acid as a carbon source. The synthesized CQDs were characterized using different spectroscopic and microscopic methods. The quantum yield of the produced CQDs was studied and revealed a value of as high as 53.3%. The emission of CQDs at 534 nm after excitation at 371 nm was quantitatively quenched in presence of meloxicam (MLX), and revealed a calibration plot with a good linearity relationship over a concentration range of 1.5–30.0 μM and a lower quantitation and detection limits of 0.74 and 0.24 μM, respectively. The proposed method was utilized for the estimation of MLX in different pharmaceutical preparations, including tablets, capsules, and ampoules with accepted % recovery values (98.7–101.0). The developed method was extended for the content uniformity testing of tablets and capsules dosage units according to British Pharmacopeia requirements. Owing to the high sensitivity of the proposed approach, MLX was successfully determined in spiked human plasma samples. The developed method was validated according to ICH Q2(R1) guidelines. The current study introduces the first CQD-based spectrofluorimetric method for the estimation of the studied drug without the need for any derivatizing reagents or drastic chemical conditions. In order to assess the environmental sustainability of the developed approach, AGREE, GAPI, Eco-Scale, and NEMI metric tools were employed. They revealed that the developed approach is excellent green, suggesting its usage as an environmentally-friendly alternative for the routine assay of the investigated drug.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly fluorescent water-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal-assisted synthesis procedure utilizing ascorbic acid as a carbon source. The synthesized CQDs were characterized using different spectroscopic and microscopic methods. The quantum yield of the produced CQDs was studied and revealed a value of as high as 53.3%. The emission of CQDs at 534 nm after excitation at 371 nm was quantitatively quenched in presence of meloxicam (MLX), and revealed a calibration plot with a good linearity relationship over a concentration range of 1.5–30.0 μM and a lower quantitation and detection limits of 0.74 and 0.24 μM, respectively. The proposed method was utilized for the estimation of MLX in different pharmaceutical preparations, including tablets, capsules, and ampoules with accepted % recovery values (98.7–101.0). The developed method was extended for the content uniformity testing of tablets and capsules dosage units according to British Pharmacopeia requirements. Owing to the high sensitivity of the proposed approach, MLX was successfully determined in spiked human plasma samples. The developed method was validated according to ICH Q2(R1) guidelines. The current study introduces the first CQD-based spectrofluorimetric method for the estimation of the studied drug without the need for any derivatizing reagents or drastic chemical conditions. In order to assess the environmental sustainability of the developed approach, AGREE, GAPI, Eco-Scale, and NEMI metric tools were employed. They revealed that the developed approach is excellent green, suggesting its usage as an environmentally-friendly alternative for the routine assay of the investigated drug.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Meloxicam (MLX), 4-hydroxy-2-methyl-(5-methyl-2-thiazolyl)-2H-1,2-benzothiazine-3- carboxamide-1,1-dioxide (Fig. 1 ), is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) from the enolic acid family that has been discovered to inhibit COX-2. MLX is a drug that is used to treat osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and other joint disorders (Xu et al., 2014). The treatment of dental or post-surgical pain is one example of its off-label usage. MLX has also been investigated for the treatment of neuropathic pain in addition to the aforementioned uses (Bekker et al., 2018). Due to its medicinal significance, it is important determine its concentration in its dosage forms as well as biological fluids. The literature survey revealed that MLX has been determined spectrophotometrically directly (Bebawy, 1998) or via derivatization with different reagents; sodium nitroprusside and 1,10-phenanthroline (Gurupadayya et al., 2013), 0.1 M NaOH and 5% ferric chloride or with 10% trisodium citrate solution (Dhandapani et al., 2010), safranin T using both spectrophotometric as well as fluorimetric methods (Hassan, 2002). Moreover, chromatographic methods with UV detection permitted MLX analysis in numerous dosage forms and biological matrices (Joseph-Charles and Bertucat, 1999a, 1999b; Velpandian et al., 2000; Sane and Surve, 2000). Therefore, it is essential to develop a simple, sensitive, and green method for its determination without the need for pre-derivatization, large volume of organic solvents or complicated instrumentation.

The spectrofluorimetric method provides a green, simple, and cost-effective analytical approach contrary to liquid chromatographic ones that require expensive equipment, a large volume of harmful solvents, experienced personnel, and lengthy analysis run time (Chen et al., 1990). Likewise, it has advantages over the derivatization-based spectroscopic methods since they consume considerable time in derivatization, use not eco-friendly reagents, and generate lower sensitivity. The purpose of this study was to offer a novel, green, and simple method with no derivatization steps or requiring sophisticated instruments.

The developed technique relied on the direct production of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through one-pot hydrothermal-assisted approach utilizing simple, green, and available starting materials, including ascorbic acid (AA) as a carbon source in a combination of water and polyethylene glycol (PEG). Then, the obtained CQDs have been used as a fluorescent nano-sensor for direct MLX assay through quantitative quenching of the native fluorescence of CQDs.

CQDs are a brand-new family of luminous nanomaterials with a size range of 2–10 nm. They are biodegradable, inexpensive to synthesis, and have exceptional fluorescence performance. They have optical and electrical properties dependent on size; hence they attracted a lot of attention recently. High photo-stability, eco-friendliness, chemical inertness, brilliant fluorescence, broad excitation bandwidths, narrow emission spectra, bio-applications, and resistance to metabolic breakdown are only a few of the distinctive and promising qualities of CQDs (Molaei, 2019, 2020; Magdy et al., 2022a). Therefore, CQDs have been employed as biosensors for detection and determination of many chemical, biological, and pharmaceutical compounds in different matrices due to their great selectivity and high sensitivity with nanomolar detection limits (Chen et al., 2018a; Li et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2019, 2021; Xu et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2022; Alossaimi et al., 2023a, 2023b; El Hamd et al., 2024; Magdy et al., 2021a, 2022b, 2023a, 2023b, 2024a, 2024b).

Traditional semi-conductor-based dots are more harmful than CQDs due to their toxic metal components such as cadmium, zinc, copper, and lead (Kirchner et al., 2005; Lovrić et al., 2005). The toxicity of Cd- and Pd-based quantum dots has attracted a lot of interest; since the toxicity of these quantum dots conceptually limits their applicability. Electrochemical oxidation (Li et al., 2011; Zheng et al., 2009), chemical oxidation (Sun et al., 2006; Dong et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2007), hydrothermal strategies (Magdy et al., 2022b, 2022c; Wang et al., 2011), microwave-assisted methods (Magdy et al., 2023a), and carbonizing organics techniques (Krysmann et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2009) have all been recognized for the manufacturing of new fluorescent CQDs. The use of pricey equipment, like microwave ovens, that operate at extremely high temperatures or complex chemical reactions that take place in harsh environments like boiling with concentrated acid, as in the carbonization of organics will be costly and time-consuming, which will lessen the approach's environmental impact. The aforementioned disadvantages limit the applicability of these techniques and make them useless.

In the current study, the preparation of dual-emitting CQDs was performed using AA as the carbon source in combination with water and PEG via a one-pot hydrothermal reaction. The AA-capped blue emitters and alcohol-capped yellow emitters were behind the formation of dual-emitting carbon dots. The optical characteristics of the yellow emitters are (λ ex = 371 nm, λ em = 534 nm), while those of the blue emitters are (λ ex = 330 nm, λ em = 410 nm) due to the various ligands in either of them (Liu et al., 2015). The generated dots were proposed as a luminescent probe for MLX analysis, as its addition resulted in quenching of CQDs' native fluorescence.

The analytical community has shown interest in the green fluorescence spectroscopy (FS) approach as a means of substituting ecologically unfavorable analytical procedures with cleaner ones, given the health and environmental dangers connected with the solvents frequently employed in FS (Tobiszewski et al., 2010). A greener FS method can be implemented by decreasing solvent usage, replacing hazardous and toxic organic solvents with less hazardous and environmentally friendly ones, and recycling solutions by scaling up to large-scale preparative analytical techniques (Welch et al., 2010; Płotka et al., 2013). Using the Analytical Eco-scale (Gałuszka et al., 2012), National Environmental Methods Index Label (NEMI) (Tobiszewski et al., 2015), Green Analytical Procedure Index (GAPI) (Płotka-Wasylka, 2018), and Analytical GREEnness (AGREE) (Pena-Pereira et al., 2020), the proposed study's level of greenness was examined.

Fisher Scientific UK, located in Loughborough, Leicestershire, UK, supplied acetonitrile, ethanol, and methanol in HPLC quality. Boric acid, sodium acetate trihydrate, sodium hydroxide, and 96% glacial acetic acid were all provided by the El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co., Cairo, Egypt. AA and PEG were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. British Pharmacopeia (BP) guidelines (The British Pharmacopoeia, 2017) were followed for the preparation of 0.2 M Acetate and borate buffers, which span the pH ranges of (3.7–5.7) and (6-10), respectively. ADWIA, Cairo, Egypt graciously provided MLX (99.4% purity). The Boehringer Ingelheim Pharma, Germany-made Mobic® 7.5 mg Tablets (batch No. 007726) and the ADWIA, Cairo, Egypt-produced Anti-COX II® 7.5 mg ampoules and capsules, batches No. 201237 and 2003124, respectively, were purchased from a local Pharmacy. The human plasma sample was obtained from the Blood Bank of Mansoura University Hospital, Mansoura, Egypt and was kept frozen at −20 °C till used after gentle thawing. 0.1 mM stock solution of MLX was prepared in methanol, then various concentrations (1.5–30.0 μM) were prepared from the stock solution through dilution with the same solvent. The prepared solutions were found to be stable for at least two weeks when kept in the refrigerator.

The synthesis of CQDs was performed following the previously reported hydrothermal method (Abdel Hamid et al., 2022) as follows: 0.8 g AA was dissolved in 10 mL of PEG and 10 mL of water. The mixture was stirred until it produced a homogeneous solution. The solution was then refluxed at 170 °C for 3 h till fluorescent CQDs are formed (the solution color switched from colorless to dark orange) (Abdel Hamid et al., 2022). Subsequently, it was allowed to cool before being used. The prepared CQDs solution was kept at 4 °C for further use.

Freeze-drying of CQDs solution (Alpha 1–2 LD plus, Germany) was performed at −65 °C under vacuum for 3 days to produce solid CQDs. The production yield (PY) was calculated after weighing the prepared solid. The following equation (1) was used to determine PY (Zheng et al., 2020):
(Eq.1)
P Y =

C Q D s

)
× 100
Where, mCQDs represents the CQDs solid mass, and ms represents the precursors mass (AA and PEG).

Into a set of 10 mL volumetric flasks, 80.0 μL of CQDs were quantitatively transferred, then aliquots of MLX stock solution were added to cover the concentration range of 1.5–30.0 μM. Finally, the flasks were completed to the mark using distilled water. The fluorescence intensity was measured at 534 nm, after excitation at 371 nm. A blank experiment was conducted similarly. The quenching values (F0–F)/F0 were plotted against MLX final concentrations to obtain the calibration graph, then a regression equation was obtained.

Ten Mobic® tablets were weighed, ground, and thoroughly mixed. 50 mL of methanol were added to a tiny conical flask containing precisely weighed 0.0035 g of the powder. The resulting solutions were sonicated for 30 min then filtered into a 100 mL volumetric flask and completed to the mark with methanol to obtain a solution with the final concentration of 0.1 mM, which was further diluted with distilled water to attain the working solutions (9.0, 12.0, 15.0, 20.0 μM). The fluorescence intensity was measured at 534 nm, after excitation at 371 nm with a blank experiment conducted similarly. The nominal contents of tablets were determined utilizing the relevant regression equation.

Ten Anti-cox II® capsules were emptied, well-mixed, and weighed. Accurately weighed amounts of the powder equivalent to 0.0035 g were transferred into a small conical flask and 50 mL of methanol were added. Following 30 min of sonication, the resulting solutions were filtered into a 100 mL volumetric flask and completely filled with methanol to yield a 0.1 mM final concentration. To create the working solutions (9.0, 12.0, 15.0, 20.0 μM), these solutions were subsequently further diluted with distilled water. Then, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 534 nm, after excitation at 371 nm. A blank experiment was conducted similarly. Using the relevant regression equation, we computed the nominal contents of capsules.

Five Anti-Cox II® ampoules' contents were homogeneously combined. Into a 100-mL volumetric flask, then a precise volume of the mixed solution's equivalent of 0.0035g of MLX was transferred. Methanol was added to the mark to obtain a solution with a 0.1 mM final concentration, which was further diluted with distilled water to attain the working solutions (9.0, 12.0, 15.0, 20.0 μM). The fluorescence intensity was measured at 534 nm, after excitation at 371 nm with a blank experiment conducted similarly. The appropriate regression equation was used to calculate the nominal content of ampoules.

The official BP guidelines were followed to ensure uniformity of dosage units tablets and capsules (The British Pharmacopoeia, 2017). Ten individual tablets and capsules were subjected to the same methodology mentioned under sections 2.4.2.1 and 2.4.2.2., respectively for the content uniformity testing. The formula used to determine the acceptance value (AV) was AV = |M ￚ X−.| + ks, in which M stands for the reference value, X− for the mean of individual contents, k for the acceptability constant, and s for the sample standard deviation. If the AV of 10 tablets or capsules is less than or equal to the maximum AV permitted by BP (L1; 15.000 unless otherwise stated), the tablets or capsules pass the content uniformity test (The British Pharmacopoeia, 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MLX in spiked plasma
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 mL aliquots of human plasma were vortexed for 1 min after being spiked with increasing volumes of MLX stock solution (0.1 mM) into centrifugation tubes. For the precipitation of plasma proteins, up to 2 mL of methanol was used. After centrifuging the tubes for approximately 20 min at 3600 rpm, they were vortexed for 1 min 16.0 μL of CQDs aliquots were transferred to a 10-mL volumetric flask, then 1.0 mL of supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μm syringe and completed to 2.0 mL with distilled water. The final concentrations were 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, and 2.8 μM. A blank experiment was run concurrently, and all the analyses were performed following the procedure mentioned under Section ''2.4.1.".

ce:list-item.ce:para: The fluorescence measurements were conducted using an Agilent Technologies® Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, Santa Clara, CA 95,051, United States, set to smoothing factor of 20, high voltage mode 800 V, and scanning range of 200–500 nm with slit width of 5 nm.
The UV-1601 PC Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) was used to investigate the UV–Vis spectrophotometric measurements.

Thermo Fisher Scientific Nicolet - iS10 FT-IR Spectrometer (168 Third Avenue Waltham, MA, USA) was used to identify the FT-IR spectra. The device has Ge/KBr beam splitter and a DTGS detector that ranged in size from 4000 to 1000 cm−1. At a resolution of 4 cm−1, 32 scans were used to record all measurements.
A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) JEM-2100 at 200 Kv was used to investigate the morphology of the produced CQDs (JEOL, Tokyo).
For pH measurements and adjustment, Consort pH-Meter, Belgium was used.
Centrifuge Model 2-16P (Germany) and IVM-300p (Taiwan) Vortex, were used for plasma disassembling and mixing, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield determination of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Precisely, two 10 mL volumetric flasks were filled with 100.0 μL of each aqueous standard solution of CQDs (test solution) and quinine sulfate solution (indicator solution). For the test and the indicator solutions, three absorbance scans were carried out, and the average absorbance values for each chemical were computed. At λ ex 371 nm, the emission spectra of the indicator and test solutions were measured three times. The adjusted fluorescence spectra were used to calculate the values of the integration peaks.

The spectrofluorimetric assessment of MLX was employed in the developed method due to its straightforwardness, quickness, sensitivity, and affordability. This work used AA and a water-alcohol mixture as the starting materials for the hydrothermal approach that is recognized for producing green and eco-friendly CQDs. The emission fluorescence spectrum of CQDs was obtained at 534 nm after excitation at 371 nm. The quantitative quenching of the native fluorescence of CQDs by the studied drug was used for the determination of its concentration. This work represents the first spectrofluorimetric approach to determine MLX directly with no need for preliminary derivatization, which represents the novelty of this work. The novelty also extends to the simplicity and greenness of the synthesis of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and mechanism of CQDs formation
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of dual-emitting CQDs was performed using AA as the carbon source in combination with water and polyethylene glycol via a one-pot hydrothermal reaction. The AA-capped blue emitters and alcohol-capped yellow emitters were behind the formation of dual-emitting carbon dots. The optical characteristics of the yellow emitters are (λ ex = 371 nm, λ em = 534 nm), while those of the blue emitters are (λ ex = 330 nm, λ em = 410 nm) due to the various ligands in either of them (Liu et al., 2015). The excessive alcohol capped the CQDs and formed the ligand to produce yellow emitters (Fig. 2 ). The produced CQDs were found to be stable for 14 days at 4 °C.

HRTEM was used to analyze the fluorescent probe's particle size and shape. Using this technique, the sample was mounted on a Cu-grid that had a carbon coating with roughly 200 meshes, and HRTEM was run at 200 kV. CQDs were found to be spherical, and most particles are distributed in the range of 1.5–4.5 nm, with an average diameter of 3.21 nm, which confirms the formation of particles in nano diameters, as shown in Fig. 3 .

A maximal UV–Vis absorption peak was shown in CQDs between 360 and 500 nm, with λ max at about 371 nm. This peak was attributed to surface states trapping excited state energy, which led afterward to high emissions (Anilkumar et al., 2011).

FT-IR investigation was performed to study the significant functional groups on the CQDs' surface (Fig. 4 ). The presence of the O–H group was confirmed by a peak at 3417 cm−1. The stretching vibrations of C–H and CO were shown by additional peaks at 2947 cm−1, and 1652 cm−1, respectively. The C–O–C symmetric and asymmetric vibrations were indicated by the peaks at 1085 cm−1 and 1043 cm−1. The water solubility of the prepared CQDs was attributed to the presence of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups (Coates, 2000).

The fluorescence characteristics of CQDs were thoroughly investigated. Different excitation wavelengths were studied. CQDs had two excitation wavelengths λ ex1 = 330 nm, and λ ex2 = 371 nm. However, excitation at λ ex2 = 371 nm revealed higher fluorescence intensity. Thus, the aqueous solution of CQDs and MLX was studied at 534 nm after excitation of 371 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 5 .
The PY of CQDs was also calculated as mentioned under Section ''2.3.'' and was found to be 21 ± 3 wt%.

Numerous mechanisms can be attributed to CQDs' interaction with drugs, for instance inner filter effect (IFE), and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), besides static and dynamic interaction. IFE, one of the non-irradiation energy conversions, is caused by absorbers in the detection system absorbing the excitation and/or emission radiation of fluorophores (Chen et al., 2018b; Kumar Panigrahi and Kumar Mishra, 2019; Tang et al., 2019). IFE would be manifested efficiently only if the absorbers' absorption spectra overlap with the excitation and/or emission spectra of the fluorophore. IFE has two types, primary IFF (pIFE) and secondary IFE (sIFE), where the pIFE refers to the absorption of fluorophores' excitation light and sIFE refers to the absorption of emission light by absorbers (Kumar Panigrahi and Kumar Mishra, 2019). IFE-based sensing strategy is an effective method for converting analytical absorption signals to fluorescence signals. FRET is a phenomenon in which energy is transferred between molecules through dipole-dipole interactions. This transfer occurs from the excited state of the donor molecule to the acceptor molecule, without the emission of radiation. The creation of resonance energy transfer is dependent on the degree of spectral overlap. The emission spectra of the donor must overlap with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor. Furthermore, the difference in energy between the lowest energy level and the first electronic excited state of the donor and acceptor must be compatible. Other significant mechanisms are static and dynamic quenching, which are deduced using the Stern-Volmer equation. The static quenching occurs due to the ground state complex formation, while in dynamic quenching, the quencher (MLX) moves in the excited state and comes into contact with the fluorophore. If providing kq (quenching rate constant) greater than the maximum diffusion rate constant, then the quenching mechanism is proved to be static instead of dynamic and vice versa (Manikandan and Lee, 2022).

As illustrated in Fig. 6 , the CQDs exhibited high fluorescence that is noticeably reduced upon addition of increasing concentrations of MLX (1.5, 3.0, 9.0, 12.0, 20.0, 30.0 μM). A methodical approach was used to identify the potential mechanism(s) by which the studied drug quenches CQDs' fluorescence. First, the excitation and emission spectra of CQDs as well as the UV absorption spectra of the quencher drug were examined. Fig. 7 verified that IFE was presumable due to the substantial overlap between the excitation spectra of CQDs and the UV absorption spectra of MLX. To further elucidate this mechanism, the fluorescence intensity measurement of CQDs in the presence of increasing MLX concentrations was corrected for potential IFE using Eq. (2) (Abd Elhaleem et al., 2022).
Eq. (2)
F corr

+

Aex and Aem: The quencher absorbance values at λ ex and λ em of the fluorescent probe, respectively, Fobs: the observed fluorescence intensity, and Fcorr: the corrected fluorescence intensity.

The suppressed efficiency (% E) for both the corrected and observed fluorescence was then estimated using Eq. (3):
Eq. (3) %E = [1-(Fcorr/Fobs]*100

Fig. 8 of the corrected and observed fluorescence of the CQDs-quencher as a % E against the quencher concentration [M] indicates that the quenching of fluorescence is mostly attributed to the IFE. The remaining portion of the quenching mechanism might be controlled by other mechanism(s), including static or dynamic quenching. An additional difference between the two mechanisms is in their reliance on temperature. In dynamic quenching, raising the temperature leads to a rise in the Stern-Volmer constant (KSV), whereas in static quenching, it causes a drop. The investigation of potential quenching mechanism was conducted utilizing the Stern-Volmer equation (Magdy et al., 2021b):
Eq. (4) F0/F = 1+Ksv[Q]
Q: MLX concentration [M].
KSV: the Stern-Volmer constant.
F0 and F: the relative fluorescence intensities of CQDs in the absence and presence of MLX, respectively.

The developed method was executed at various temperature settings (298, 308, 318 K) using the Stern-Volmer equation. The plot of F0/F against the MLX concentration in μM is shown in Fig. 9 . The KSV values were 2.25 × 104, 2.05 × 104, and 1.87 × 104, at temperatures of 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K, respectively. From the obtained results, the process of quenching is specifically attributed to static quenching due to the observed drop in the KSV values with rising temperature. Additionally, from the obtained KSV values, kq values were calculated using Eq. (5):
Eq. (5) KSV = KqƮo
Kq = the quenching rate constant and Ʈo is the average lifetime of CQDs (10−8 s).

The calculated Kq values were found to be 2.25 × 1012, 2.05 × 1012, and 1.87 × 1012, L.mol−1.s−1, respectively, which were considerably greater than the maximum diffusion rate constant (2.0 × 1010 L mol−1.s−1); further confirming the static quenching. Accordingly, the MLX-induced fluorescence quenching of the CQDs' fluorescence is attributed to IFE as well as static quenching.

As mentioned under Section ''3.2.2.'', the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 4) revealed that the prepared CQDs surface function groups contain hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. The mechanism of interaction between CQDs and MLX can be attributed to the interaction between MLX and these functional groups, which results in quenching of the native fluorescence of CQDs.

Differing volumes of CQDs were tested in the range of (50.0–150.0 μL) to obtain high fluorescence intensity. After several trials, 80.0 μL of CQDs in an aqueous solution was found to be optimum as it gave the highest intensity with reasonable robustness.

It has long been understood that a solution's pH has an impact on how strongly CQDs fluoresce as well as how well they interact with the studied drug (Zhang et al., 2011). Using 0.2 M acetate and borate buffers covering pH ranges of 3.7–5.7 and 6–9, respectively, the pH effect on F0–F of CQDs fluorescence intensity by MLX was examined. There was no discernible effect of pH variations on the CQDs' strength of quenching. As a result, the buffer solution was not required in this procedure, offering the simplicity and environmental friendliness of the suggested method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of the incubation period
ce:section.ce:para: Due to its significant influence on fluorescence intensity, the incubation period was also investigated from 1 to 30 min. It was evaluated after the studied drug was added to CQDs. This method has the advantage of saving time because the reaction between the tested drug and CQDs was found to be instantaneous, taking just a few seconds to achieve, and the fluorescence intensity was stable for at least 30 min.
The suggested technique was according to using the International Conference on Harmonization (ICH) Q2 (R1) guidelines (ICH Guidelines, 2005).

Plotting F0–F/Fo against the final drug concentrations in μM was the method used to create the calibration curve after the fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs with and without the drug under study were obtained under ideal conditions. The calibration curve was rectilinear for the concentration range of 1.5–30.0 μM with a correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9999, as illustrated in Table 1 .

The linear regression could be expressed by the subsequent equation:
(F0–F)/F0 = 0.0154CMLX + 0.0406
F0 and F: CQDs fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of the studied drug.
CMLX: the concentration of meloxicam in μM
A value approaching the unity of the correlation coefficient indicates excellent linearity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of quantitation (LOQ) and limit of detection (LOD)
ce:section.ce:para:
LOD and LOQ were evaluated in agreement with ICH Q2R1 guidelines (ICH Guidelines, 2005) using the following equations:
Eq. (6) LOQ = 10 Sa/b
Eq. (7) LOD = 3.3 Sa/b
b: the calibration curve slope, Sa: the intercept of the standard deviation.
Table 1 indicates that the suggested approach is adequately sensitive based on the obtained LOD and LOQ, allowing its application for analysis of the studied drug in biological fluids.

By comparing the suggested technique's results on raw materials and different pharmaceutical preparations to those of the comparative method (Dhandapani et al., 2010), the studied technique's accuracy was demonstrated. In terms of accuracy and precision, Table 2 showed no appreciable variations in the two techniques' performance. This was validated by the Student's t-test and F test, respectively (Miller and Miller, 2005).

The approach's precision was assessed using intraday and interday precisions. Each drug was evaluated at three different concentrations, each with 3 replicates. The suggested method has a % RSD and SD less than 2.0, as shown in Table 3 .

The robustness as crucial validation criteria was investigated. It shows how little changes in certain parameters can have an impact on the drug's fluorimetric analysis. Table 4 illustrates that the addition of 80.0 μL ± 1 of CQDs had no discernible effects on the percentage recovery and % RSD readings.

When the recommended procedure's specificity was investigated, it was discovered that the endogenous plasma matrix components and commonly used excipients added to ampoules, capsules, and tablets had no impact on the fluorescence signals acquired in this investigation, as shown in Tables 5–7
. The tolerance limits of various co-administered and/or co-formulated medications which are likely to interact with the studied drug were investigated. These drugs including pantoprazole, diacerein, pregabalin, glucosamine, and paracetamol, were determined to examine the method's specificity. No interference occurred till 200.0 μg/mL. High-achieved tolerance limit values demonstrate the developed method's outstanding specificity. Moreover, the method's specificity was confirmed by its ability to determine the studied drug in human plasma samples with high % recoveries (99.00–100.47) and low % RSD (≤0.648) values without interference from the complicated plasma matrices.

The studied approach worked well for measuring MLX in different pharmaceutical preparations. The previously derived regression equation was used to calculate the % recoveries of the proposed drug in various pharmaceuticals. The obtained results were compared to those of the reported approach (Dhandapani et al., 2010) using Student's t-test and the variance ratio F-test. As shown in Table 5, there is no appreciable difference in accuracy and precision between the two approaches based on the computed t and F values, respectively (Miller and Miller, 2005).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the proposed method for content uniformity testing of tablets and capsules
ce:section.ce:para: The present technique exhibited excellent sensitivity and selectivity, enabling the quantification of drug concentrations in low-dose tablets and capsules. Using the procedures described in Sections 2.4.2.1 and 2.4.2.2, we analyzed ten Mobic® tablets and Anti-cox II® capsules separately. The consistency of the content of tablets or capsules was evaluated according to the established requirements of the BP (The British Pharmacopoeia, 2017). The computed AV was below the maximum permissible AV (L1) indicated by BP (Table 6).

The reported MLX Cmax of 1.7 μg/ml (Bae et al., 2007) is within the operational range of the examined methodology. The suggested method effectively analyzes MLX in plasma with strong selectivity and sensitivity, unhindered by endogenous components of the plasma. According to the data collected, the mean percent recovery and standard deviation of MLX in human plasma were 100.00% ± 0.97, as shown in Table 7.

The quantum yield of a fluorophore is an intrinsic characteristic. The relative fluorescence quantum yield of fluorescent substances can be easily calculated using a quantum yield reference. A reference fluorescence quantum yield that absorbs and emits in the same wavelength range as the drug is essential for determining the fluorescence quantum yield of the drug under investigation. Specific measurement conditions (e.g., solvent/matrix, excitation wavelength, temperature, chromophore concentration) must be selected. Quinine sulfate, which has a fluorescence quantum yield of 0.546 in dil. H2SO4 (Eaton, 1988), was employed as the reference in the single-point technique (Williams et al., 1983) for determining the Q of CQDs. The absorbance at λ ex should be less than 0.1 in order to prevent the effects of inner filters. Each sample's absorbance and fluorescence spectra were computed simultaneously to reduce experimental error. Using the corresponding equation (8), the fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs in methanol was determined to be 53.3% (Williams et al., 1983).
Eq. (8)
Q u
=
Q R

Where: Iu, IR: CQDs and quinine sulfate integrated fluorescence intensities, QR, Qu: the FL quantum yield of quinine sulfate/dil. H2SO4 and CQDs/H2O, respectively, ƞ: the solvent refractive index, and Au, AR: CQDs and quinine sulfate absorbance values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the developed method's greenness
ce:section.ce:para: Analysts plays crucial role in preventing dangerous organic waste and solvents from chemical and pharmaceutical processes from harming the environment and people. Green Analytical Chemistry (GAC) is a topic that is frequently updated and modified. In the current study, numerous greenness measures, including Eco-scale (Gałuszka et al., 2012), National Environmental Methods Index Label (NEMI) (Tobiszewski et al., 2015), Green Analytical Procedure Index (GAPI) (Płotka-Wasylka, 2018), and Analytical GREEnness (AGREE) (Pena-Pereira et al., 2020), were used to assess the greenness profile and environmental friendliness of the developed method.

The ecology of the approach was evaluated using an Eco-scale Penalty paradigm (Gałuszka et al., 2012). It is based on the calculation of a numerical score to get a final value indicating the greenness of the method, where 100 is the ideal green procedure. From the total of 100 points, penalty points are subtracted for each negative impact on the environment (like hazardous chemicals used, waste generation, and high energy consumption), where Analytical Eco-Scale = 100 - total penalty points. The higher the value, the more environmentally beneficial the method is. Rather than using organic solvents, water was used in the suggested method. More than 0.1 kWh of heating or power-hungry operations per sample were also steered clear of. The suggested strategy received a score of 94 on the Eco-scale, indicating that it was an excellent green method (Table 8 ).

Although the NEMI (Tobiszewski et al., 2015) is the most well-known and uncomplicated reading method in the field, another approach uses it instead, but it is labor-intensive, has a number of drawbacks, and does not account for the amount of work needed to create its circular pictogram. This pictogram consists of four parts, each colored green or white based on how well the relevant criteria are fulfilled. The following four requirements are stated: the pH range should be between 2 and 12, with the exception of the reagents employed, and it shouldn't be on the lists of hazardous wastes or poisonous, persistent, or bioaccumulative compounds. Furthermore, the net waste volume must not exceed 50 gm or L (Tobiszewski et al., 2015). It was found that the developed approach satisfies all requirements for certification as a green process, based on the previously established standards (Table 8).

The GAPI (Anastas and Kirchhoff, 2002) is a recent software that has become available. It is regarded as one of the most innovative ideas for determining the environmental friendliness. The difficulties with the earlier listed tools are satisfactorily overcome by this tool. The three-level color scale used by the GAPI tool to classify the degree of greenness employs pictograms at each stage of the analysis process. The developed spectrofluorimetric method was evaluated to meet the majority of GAPI standards by the use of GAPI pictogram. Because they stand for untreated waste and off-line sampling, respectively, boxes 1 and 15 are colored red. The basic sample preparation technique and 10 mL waste are the reasons why boxes 5 and 14 are yellow. GAPI has also been used with the solvent methanol (red) and microextraction (yellow) technique for drug determination in biological matrices (Table 8).

Recently, a brand-new technique (AGREE) was developed to give a fair evaluation of the environmental impact of the suggested technology (Pena-Pereira et al., 2020). The twelve segments of AGREE's clock-shaped graph correspond to the twelve tenets of GAC. Each element represents a distinct mindset and is colored (red, yellow, and green) based on how environmentally friendly the analytical process is. On a graph with a scale from 0 to 1, the overall assessment value is displayed (Pena-Pereira et al., 2020; Sajid and Płotka-Wasylka, 2022). The visual representation in Table 8 suggests that the developed method is generally consistent with the green parameters, since they have minimal impact on both human health and the environment.

Accordingly, the four greenness metrics demonstrated the excellent greenness and ecofriendliness of the develop method, which allows its application as a green alternative for determination of the studied drug in its dosage forms and biological fluids.

A unique and ecologically friendly methodology for the analysis of MLX in different dosage forms and content uniformity tests was conducted, based on the quenching of CQDs' native fluorescence. The used CQDs were created using an eco-friendly, simple, and affordable approach that made use of widely accessible starting ingredients and a single hydrothermal process to create a very water-soluble, biocompatible CQDs-based probe with a size of about 3.21 nm. Prior to this, the synthesized fluorescent probe was characterized using a variety of methods, including FT-IR, fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, and HRTEM. The developed sensor's fluorescence was quantitatively decreased by the investigated drug, which allowed for a quick and reliable fluorescence analytical technique for the investigation of the investigated medication. The examined method has a wide linearity range of 1.5–30.0 M and was sensitive, accurate, and precise. For the evaluation of MLX in various pharmaceuticals in addition to in spiking human plasma, the proposed approach was adjusted, with satisfactory % recoveries and an acceptable value for %RSD. This is the first technique for MLX measurement that employs direct spectrofluorimetric analysis without the necessity for a prior derivatization step or the use of chromogenic chemicals. The future perspectives for the developed method can include the use of rapid microwave-assisted synthetic approaches for the synthesis of CQDs, which may take only few seconds or minutes, instead of the time consuming synthetic methods. In addition, the synthesis can be performed using the readily available green plant sources, which can positively affect the method's cost and ecofriendliness. Moreover, the optimization of the synthesis of CQDs and the factors affecting the developed method could be better performed using an analytical quality-by-design approach rather than the univariate optimization techniques, which could save time, effort, and resources. The use of the synthesized CQDs for analysis of other structurally related drugs in their pharmaceutical dosage forms and biological fluids could also be a subject of upcoming studies.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
